---
title: "The **Impeccable** Design System"
subtitle: "Typography-First · OKLCH Color · Semantic Structure"
author: "impeccable-quarto"
date: "2025-01-15"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: ../themes/impeccable.scss
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    center: false
    embed-resources: true
---

## Design Philosophy {.center}

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
::: {.keybox}
**Typography First**

Every design decision flows from the type system.
Font choice, scale, and hierarchy set the visual tone
before color or layout enter the conversation.
:::
:::
::: {}
::: {.keybox}
**Objectively Scored**

Presentations earn a score from 0--100.
Anti-patterns are detected and penalized automatically ---
no more "looks good enough" reviews.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide introduces the two pillars of the impeccable design system.
Typography-first means we pick fonts, set a modular scale, and define hierarchy before anything else.
Objective scoring means every deck gets a numerical quality gate --- from Failing (0--59) to Impeccable (95--100).
Transition: "Let's start with how we handle color."
:::

---

## OKLCH Color Palette

All colors live in the perceptually uniform OKLCH space --- no more hex guesswork.

::: {.infobox}
**Primary** · `oklch(0.45 0.18 265)` --- Deep Indigo for main accents and links
:::

::: {.warningbox}
**Accent** · `oklch(0.72 0.16 85)` --- Marigold for highlights and attention
:::

::: {.tipbox}
**Secondary** · `oklch(0.55 0.12 195)` --- Warm Teal for supporting elements
:::

::: {.notes}
OKLCH is a perceptually uniform color space --- equal numeric steps produce equal visual steps.
This eliminates the common problem where hex colors that "look close" in code appear jarringly different on screen.
Our palette has three core roles: primary (indigo), secondary (teal), and accent (marigold).
Semantic colors (success, warning, danger, info) extend these for content boxes.
Transition: "But what about black and white?"
:::

---

## Tinted Neutrals

::: {.comparison}
::: {.compare-left}
**Avoid: Pure Extremes**

- Pure `#000000` feels harsh and clinical
- Pure `#FFFFFF` glares under projection
- Zero warmth, zero personality
:::
::: {.compare-right}
**Use: Tinted Neutrals**

- Near-black: `oklch(0.12 0.015 265)`
- Near-white: `oklch(0.96 0.012 265)`
- Subtle indigo hue for cohesion
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Tinted neutrals carry a subtle hue from the primary palette --- in our case, indigo at hue 265.
This creates visual warmth and ties the neutral scale to the overall color system.
Pure black and white are penalized in quality scoring (MAJ-05: -3 per instance).
The neutral scale has 10 steps from near-black to near-white, all with chroma between 0.008 and 0.015.
Transition: "Now let's look at the type system that drives everything."
:::

---

## Typography Stack

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
### Display --- Plus Jakarta Sans

Headings and titles use a geometric sans-serif
with strong character and excellent weight range.

### Body --- Source Sans 3

Body text uses an open, readable typeface
optimized for both screen and projection.
:::
::: {}
### Code --- JetBrains Mono

```python
def score(deck: Path) -> int:
    """Start at 100, deduct issues."""
    return max(0, 100 - deductions)
```

### Type Scale --- 1.25 Major Third

28px base → 35px → 44px → 55px → 68px
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The three-font stack covers every content need: Plus Jakarta Sans for display, Source Sans 3 for body, JetBrains Mono for code.
The 1.25 Major Third scale ensures clear hierarchy --- each step up is 25% larger.
Our 28px base guarantees readability at projection distance, even from the back row.
Anything below 20px is flagged as an anti-pattern (MAJ-03: -5 per instance).
Transition: next section will cover semantic boxes and layout classes.
:::

---

## Semantic Boxes {.center}

Six purpose-built containers for structured content.

::: {.three-col}
::: {}
::: {.keybox}
**Key Finding**
The primary takeaway your audience remembers.
:::

::: {.methodbox}
**Methodology**
Describe process, approach, or experimental design.
:::
:::
::: {}
::: {.warningbox}
**Caveat**
Flag limitations, risks, or known issues.
:::

::: {.tipbox}
**Best Practice**
Actionable recommendations for your audience.
:::
:::
::: {}
::: {.quotebox}
"Design is not how it looks, but how it works."

[Steve Jobs]{.attribution}
:::

::: {.infobox}
**Note**
Supplementary context or background detail.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Walk through each box type and its semantic purpose. Keybox is for primary takeaways --- the thing you want the audience to remember. Methodbox describes process. Warningbox flags caveats. Tipbox offers actionable advice. Quotebox for attributed quotes. Infobox for supplementary context. Emphasize that semantic meaning drives styling --- pick the box that matches your content's intent.
:::

---

## Layout Classes {.center}

Grid systems for structured content arrangement.

::: {.two-col}
::: {}
### Two-Column Layout

Split content into equal halves --- ideal for side-by-side explanations or image-text pairs.

`.two-col` with two child divs.
:::
::: {}
### When to Use

- Contrasting two concepts
- Image alongside explanation
- Code beside its output
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.three-col}
::: {}
**1/3 Column**
Compact items
:::
::: {}
**2/3 Column**
Medium detail
:::
::: {}
**3/3 Column**
Tertiary info
:::
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Demonstrate the two main grid classes. Two-col splits evenly with a 2.5em gap --- great for compare/contrast or image-text pairs. Three-col works for triple concepts or feature lists. Mention that sidebar-left and sidebar-right variants exist for asymmetric layouts (1:2 and 2:1 ratios). The fragment at the bottom reveals the three-column example on click, showing progressive disclosure combined with layout.
:::

---

## Before vs After {.center}

Why a design system matters.

::: {.comparison}
::: {.compare-left}
**Before --- Generic Theme**

- Default Reveal.js styling
- No typographic hierarchy
- Pure black and white text
- Bullets without structure
- No semantic containers
:::
::: {.compare-right}
**After --- Impeccable Theme**

- OKLCH color palette with tinted neutrals
- Major Third type scale at 28px base
- Semantic boxes convey meaning
- Layout classes for visual structure
- Speaker notes on every slide
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide is the emotional pivot of the demo. Pause on the left column --- audiences have seen hundreds of generic decks. Then walk through the right column feature by feature. The comparison layout handles the visual split automatically. Stress that impeccable is not about decoration --- it is about communication quality. Every choice on the right side exists to help the audience understand faster.
:::

---

## Progressive Disclosure {.center}

Reveal content step by step to guide attention.

::: {.fragment}
**Step 1** --- Start with the core claim or context.
:::

::: {.fragment .slide-up}
**Step 2** --- Add supporting evidence or detail.
:::

::: {.fragment .scale-in}
**Step 3** --- Deliver the punchline or takeaway.
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.tipbox}
**Tip:** Use fragments sparingly --- max 3--4 per slide to maintain pacing.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Click through each fragment one at a time to demonstrate the animation styles. Default fragment is a simple fade-in. Slide-up adds vertical motion for a sense of building. Scale-in draws attention to the final point. The tipbox fragment at the end reinforces restraint --- too many fragments make a slide feel like a slideshow within a slideshow. Timing tip: pause 2--3 seconds between clicks to let the audience absorb each step.
:::

---

## YAML Frontmatter

Use this template as the default starting point for a new impeccable deck.

```yaml
---
title: "Presentation Title"
subtitle: "Subtitle if needed"
author: "Author Name"
date: "2025-01-15"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: ../themes/impeccable.scss
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    center: false
    embed-resources: true
---
```

::: {.tipbox}
**Keep These Settings**

- Point `theme` at the impeccable SCSS file.
- Keep `width: 1920` and `height: 1080` for full HD layouts.
- Enable `embed-resources: true` for portable output.
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide gives the audience a copy-pasteable starting point.
Call out the three settings that matter most in practice: the theme path, the 1920 by 1080 canvas, and embedded resources for a self-contained HTML export.
Transition: "Once the frontmatter is stable, the next customization point is the theme."
:::

---

## Theme Customization

Override variables in a custom SCSS file, then import the base theme.

```scss
/* themes/my-impeccable.scss */
$primary: oklch(0.52 0.17 230);
$font-display: "Plus Jakarta Sans", sans-serif;
$font-body: "Source Sans 3", sans-serif;
$font-code: "JetBrains Mono", monospace;

$base-font-size: 30px;
$scale-ratio: 1.25;
$h1-size: $base-font-size * pow($scale-ratio, 4);
$h2-size: $base-font-size * pow($scale-ratio, 3);

@import "impeccable";
```

::: {.methodbox}
**Override Strategy**

- Set brand variables before the import.
- Change the font stack by role: display, body, and code.
- Adjust the base size and scale ratio to reshape the hierarchy.
:::

::: {.notes}
The pattern here is simple: override tokens first, then pull in the system.
Explain that teams usually touch color, font roles, and scale before anything else, because those three controls change the deck's character without rewriting component styles.
Transition: "After the theme is set, the daily workflow comes down to a few commands."
:::

---

## Build Commands

These are the core commands for rendering, previewing, and scoring a deck.

```bash
quarto render path/to/deck.qmd
quarto preview path/to/deck.qmd
python scripts/quality_score.py path/to/deck.qmd
```

::: {.keybox}
**Most Important**

Run `quarto render path/to/deck.qmd` before review or delivery.
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide should feel operational rather than conceptual.
Lead with render as the final artifact check, mention preview for iteration, and close on the quality scorer as the automated gate that keeps decks consistent.
:::

---

## Quality Gates

Start at **100**. Every issue deducts points until the deck lands in a clear quality gate.

| Gate | Score Range |
| --- | --- |
| Failing | 0--59 |
| Needs Work | 60--79 |
| Draft | 80--84 |
| Presentable | 85--89 |
| Excellent | 90--94 |
| Impeccable | 95--100 |

::: {.warningbox}
**Common deductions**

- Overflowing content
- Text below minimum size
- Pure black or white usage
- Missing speaker notes
- Generic, unthemed slides
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide explains the gatekeeping model in one frame.
Every presentation starts at 100, then loses points for objective issues instead of subjective taste.
The threshold labels help teams decide whether a deck is blocked, reviewable, or ready to present.
Call out the most common deductions so the audience sees what usually drags a score down.
Transition: "If the score drops, it usually comes from a small set of repeat mistakes."
:::

---

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid

::: {.warningbox}
**Top 5 anti-patterns**

- Bullet Wall
- Content Dump
- Pure Black/White
- Missing Speaker Notes
- Generic Theme
:::

::: {.notes}
Keep this slide brisk and direct.
These five patterns show up across weak decks regardless of topic or audience.
The point is not style policing; it is to prevent predictable failures in readability, narrative, and polish.
Transition: "So what should someone do if they want to start using the system today?"
:::

---

## Get Started

::: {.keybox}
**Use the system, then verify the score.**

Start with the core theme at `themes/impeccable.scss` and pick a starter deck from `templates/`.
:::

::: {.quotebox}
"Design is intelligence made visible."

[Alina Wheeler]{.attribution}
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the closing call to action.
Point people to the canonical theme file first, then the template directory so adoption feels immediate.
End on the quote to reinforce that design quality is a deliberate system, not decoration.
Final close: invite the audience to build a deck, run the review pipeline, and aim for the impeccable gate.
:::
